prompt: In economics, capital means:
choices: accumulated wealth used to produce further wealth, such as tools, machinery, or money invested in a business
the total population of a country available for work
a tax levied on imported goods
the physical building where a government is seated

prompt: In economics, interest means:
choices: the price paid for the use of borrowed money over time
the emotion of curiosity a buyer feels toward a product
a legal claim of ownership over land
the portion of a crop set aside for the following year's planting

prompt: In economics, division of labor means:
choices: splitting a complex task into simpler specialized parts performed by different workers
distributing a farmer's harvest evenly among his children
the legal process of dissolving a business partnership
assigning soldiers to different regiments before a battle

prompt: In economics, a monopoly means:
choices: exclusive control of a market by a single seller, free from competition
a game played with paper money and small tokens
a tax collected only once in a person's lifetime
an agreement between two nations to trade without tariffs

prompt: In economics, supply and demand means:
choices: the relationship between how much of a good is offered for sale and how much buyers want, which together set its price
the process by which a government prints new currency
the balance of trade between two neighboring countries
the schedule by which a factory ships its goods to market

prompt: In economics, thrift means:
choices: the careful, prudent management of money, avoiding needless waste
a sudden rise in the price of a staple good
a merchant's stock of unsold goods
the interest charged by a pawnbroker

prompt: In economics, a dividend means:
choices: a portion of a company's profits paid out to its shareholders
the wage paid to a laborer at the end of each week
a fine imposed on a merchant for selling short weight
the difference between a nation's imports and exports

prompt: In economics, credit means:
choices: the trust that allows one party to supply money or goods to another without immediate payment
the total value of goods a nation produces in a year
a public record of a person's charitable donations
the fixed price set by law for a loaf of bread

prompt: A penny saved is a penny
choices: earned
spent
lost
borrowed

prompt: Time is
choices: money
silver
green
slow

prompt: Early to bed and early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and
choices: wise
tired
poor
late

prompt: God helps those who help
choices: themselves
others
no one
strangers

prompt: Little strokes fell great
choices: oaks
storms
kings
rivers

prompt: He that goes a-borrowing goes a-
choices: sorrowing
wandering
singing
resting

prompt: Well done is better than well
choices: said
planned
paid
known

prompt: A word to the wise is
choices: sufficient
wasted
forgotten
expensive

prompt: The best way to build wealth over time is to
choices: save consistently and put the surplus to productive use
spend every dollar of income as soon as it arrives
borrow as heavily as possible for daily comforts
ignore your income and expenses entirely

prompt: A young man who wishes to succeed in business should
choices: work diligently, keep his word, and live within his means
promise more than he can deliver to win customers quickly
avoid all work and wait for a fortunate inheritance
spend his capital on amusements before he has earned any

prompt: When starting to save money, it is wisest to
choices: begin with small, regular sums set aside before spending on luxuries
wait until a large windfall arrives before saving anything at all
spend freely now, trusting that saving will happen by itself later
borrow against future income to fund present pleasures

prompt: A merchant who wants the trust of his customers should
choices: deal honestly and give fair weight and measure
quietly overcharge those customers who do not check their change
make promises he has no intention of keeping
hide the true condition of his goods from buyers

prompt: If a man wishes to keep out of debt, he should
choices: spend less than he earns and keep careful accounts
buy on credit whatever catches his eye
assume that tomorrow's income will cover today's excess
avoid keeping any record of what he owes

prompt: The surest foundation of a good character is
choices: habitual honesty and diligence in one's daily work
cleverness in deceiving others without being caught
the size of one's inherited fortune
the number of idle hours one can afford each day

prompt: Before investing his savings, a prudent man will
choices: study the matter carefully and seek sound advice
trust the first stranger who promises a quick fortune
invest without inquiry, relying entirely on luck
borrow more money so as to invest a larger sum blindly

prompt: Perseverance in one's calling, more than any other single quality, tends to produce
choices: steady, lasting success
sudden and effortless riches
the admiration of idle men
an early retirement from all labor
